title: Learning Deductive Reasoning from Synthetic Corpus based on Formal Logic
authors: Terufumi Morishita, Gaku Morio, Atsuki Yamaguchi, Yasuhiro Sogawa
venue: Proceedings of the 40th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2023), PMLR 202:25254-25274, 2023 (arXiv:2308.07336, v1 2023-08-11 / v2 2023-11-12 / v3 2023-11-14)
claim: The paper argues that prior synthetic deduction corpora (RuleTaker, AACorpus) used limited or arbitrary sets of deduction rules, which caps the generalizability of any reasoning ability learned from them; by contrast, the axioms of first-order predicate logic are provably complete (any valid deduction rule is derivable from them via multistep deduction), so a corpus built from the axioms (FLD) should teach the most generalizable deductive reasoning. Empirically, even GPT-4 solves only about half of FLD's 10-shot problems by answer accuracy (and far less by strict proof accuracy), while LMs fine-tuned on FLD transfer more robustly to other deduction-rule sets and to human-authored EntailmentBank than LMs trained on RuleTaker/AACorpus-style corpora.
novelty: The authors claim FLD is 'the first to leverage formal logic theory,' i.e. the first corpus generator whose deduction rules are the axioms of first-order predicate logic (proved complete by Gödel 1930) rather than a hand-picked or arbitrary rule set (Sec 1, contributions list). This yields a generator (Table 1) that is simultaneously flexible on proof-tree depth (any), branching (any), distractor count (any, up to ~20), formula complexity (simple/complex), linguistic diversity, and choice of proof label (proved/disproved/unknown) -- properties the paper says RuleTaker and AACorpus each only partially offer. A second claimed contribution is the systematic 'ablation corpora' study (Sec 7) isolating which specific corpus-design aspects (rule diversity, tree depth, linguistic diversity, formula complexity, distractors) actually transfer benefit to LMs, which the authors say prior work had not investigated.

protocol.description: Two separate evaluation protocols are used. (1) Fine-tuned prover setting (Sec 4.1, 5.1): a T5-based 'stepwise prover' (Yang et al. 2022 architecture) is trained on a corpus's training split and generates one proof step at a time ('fact1 & fact3 -> The Earth has seasons') in a multi-step generation loop until the hypothesis is (dis-)proved, then emits a final answer token (__PROVED__/__DISPROVED__/__UNKNOWN__). This is an interactive/iterative decoding loop over one instance, not single-shot, but it is a trained generative prover, not a general LLM being probed. (2) Few-shot in-context LLM setting (Sec 4.1, 5.2): GPT-4, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and LongAlpaca-13B are evaluated with 10 in-context examples (10-shot) per query; each in-context example is 'a pair of a prompt and an output, as illustrated in Figure 1' (facts + hypothesis -> generated proof steps -> final answer). A chain-of-thought instruction is appended: 'Show me a step-by-step thought to the hypothesis based on the given set of facts.' Experiments were run for 10 different sets of in-context examples (i.e., resampled few-shot demonstration sets, not multi-turn dialogue).
protocol.observation_content: For the LLM few-shot setting: each call shows the model a fixed set of 10 (prompt, output) demonstration pairs (facts + hypothesis -> proof steps + answer) plus the CoT instruction, followed by the target instance's facts and hypothesis; the model must generate the full proof-step sequence and a final proved/disproved/unknown answer in one generation (single forward pass per instance, but with 10-shot demonstrations of the full input-output format including intermediate proof steps, so the model observes worked examples of the entire multi-step derivation, not just the label). For the fine-tuned T5 prover: the model observes one full instance (facts + hypothesis) per generation, and generates proof steps autoregressively step-by-step (each generated step conditions on all previously generated steps) until termination, so within a single instance it is an iterative/interactive generation loop against its own prior outputs, though there is no external verifier or environment feedback between steps -- it is unguided self-continuation, not an interactive multi-turn dialogue with new information injected.

representations.summary: The paper compares corpora, not literal prompt-format variants, along axes in Table 1/Table 2: deduction-rule set (implication-only vs critical-thinking vs axioms), proof-tree depth distribution (skewed 1-3/1-5 vs uniform 1-3/1-5/1-8), number of distractors (0, ~20), formula complexity (simple vs complex), and linguistic diversity (less: 1 template + 100-word vocab per POS; more: up to 5 nested templates + 5000-word vocab per POS, Appendix F.1). Effects on accuracy (few-shot proof accuracy, Sec 7): (a) linguistic diversity had little effect -- a prover trained on low-diversity RT or FLD.2 performed about as well as one trained on the high-diversity counterpart (RT.PR/FLD.3) on both corpora (Table 8: avg. 70.4 vs 70.7), because 'LMs are self-sufficient on the linguistic aspect' (Sec 7.2); (b) formula complexity mattered a lot -- a prover trained only on simple formulas (FLD.1) transferred poorly to complex formulas (FLD.2: 46.0 vs FLD.2-trained 66.8, Table 9), so complex-formula training is necessary; (c) distractors mattered a lot -- a prover trained without distractors (FLD.0) transferred poorly to the distractor-bearing corpus (FLD.2: 56.7 vs FLD.2-trained 66.8, Table 10); (d) rule-set diversity (axioms vs implication-only) was the single biggest driver of both difficulty and transfer generalizability (Table 5, Sec 6.1).
representations.note: No literal 'input representation format' (e.g., JSON vs natural language vs symbolic notation) ablation is reported; all corpora use natural-language-templated statements per Sec 3.3/Appendix E.3.

models.name: T5-base / T5-large stepwise prover (Yang et al. 2022 architecture)
models.key_results: Fully fine-tuned on all 30k examples: FLD proof accuracy 66.4, FLD* (depth up to 8) 37.7, vs RuleTaker RT 92.4 and RT.PR 93.9 (Table 3). Version-2-corpus corrected numbers: FLD 75.8 / FLD* 44.4 proof accuracy, 91.6 / 72.2 answer accuracy (Table H.25-H.26, and reproduced in Table 4 as the 'T5 (fine-tuned)' reference row: 75.8 proof-acc / 91.6 answer-acc on FLD, 44.4 / 72.2 on FLD*). Few-shot transfer trained on sFLD generalized best on average across corpora (Table 5, avg. 81.3 vs next-best RT.PR/RT.BE at 78.5). EntailmentBank transfer: FLD.D5-trained prover ~ on par with RT.D5-trained prover and both beat the no-deduction-training T5 baseline (Table 6: Task1 39.2 vs 39.4 vs 36.8; Task2 32.6 vs 32.0 vs 31.2; Task3 8.3 vs 8.2 vs 6.2).

models.name: GPT-4
strategies: chain-of-thought instruction ('Show me a step-by-step thought to the hypothesis based on the given set of facts.')
models.key_results: Table 4 (version-2 corpora), columns grouped by metric-then-corpus (confirmed by cross-referencing the T5 row 75.8/44.4/91.6/72.2 against Tables H.25-H.26, which fixes the column order as proof-acc-FLD, proof-acc-FLD*, answer-acc-FLD, answer-acc-FLD*): GPT-4 proof accuracy FLD 12.8, proof accuracy FLD* 3.2, answer accuracy FLD 52.4, answer accuracy FLD* 49.4. Abstract/Sec 1: 'Even GPT-4 can solve only half of the problems.' GPT-4 'seldom answered unknown' even when facts were insufficient (Sec 5.2).

models.name: GPT-3.5-Turbo
models.key_results: Table 4: proof accuracy FLD 0.0, proof accuracy FLD* 2.0, answer accuracy FLD 35.8, answer accuracy FLD* 37.6.

models.name: LongAlpaca-13B
models.key_results: Table 4: proof accuracy FLD 0.0, proof accuracy FLD* 0.0, answer accuracy FLD 21.2, answer accuracy FLD* 19.6 -- near or below random guess (random guess row: proof accuracy FLD/FLD* 0.0/0.0, answer accuracy FLD/FLD* 33.3/33.3).

headline_numbers: Even GPT-4 solves only about half the problems by answer accuracy in a 10-shot setting on FLD (52.4) and FLD* (49.4), and performs far worse by the stricter proof-accuracy metric (FLD 12.8, FLD* 3.2) that also checks the generated proof steps (Table 4, Sec 5.2).

Fully fine-tuned T5 prover proof accuracy: RuleTaker 92.4-93.9 vs FLD 66.4 (75.8 in corrected v2 corpus) vs FLD* (depth up to 8) 37.7 (44.4 in v2) -- FLD is substantially harder than RuleTaker despite equivalent supervision size (Table 3, Table H.25).

Few-shot transfer: a prover trained on sFLD (axiom-based, shallow) generalizes best on average (81.3) across RT/RT.PR/RT.BE/sFLD-impl/sFLD-crit/sFLD targets, versus 78.5 for RT/RT.PR-trained provers (Table 5).

Depth-wise breakdown (Table 7): with implication-only rules, shallow-trained provers generalize reasonably well to depth 4-8 (e.g., FLD-impl.0-trained: avg 53.9 vs FLD-impl.1-trained 52.3); but with the full axiom set, accuracy collapses at depth >=3 regardless of training depth (FLD.3-trained and FLD.4-trained both hit ~0% at depth 6-8), attributed to the O(A^d) explosion in proof-tree pattern count with A~10 rule choices per step.

Distractors and formula complexity each matter for transfer (necessary but not sufficient): training without distractors or with only simple formulas transfers poorly to the harder counterpart corpus (distractor-free FLD.0 -> FLD.2 drop from 76.4 to 56.7 self-vs-cross; simple FLD.1 -> FLD.2 drop from 77.9 to 46.0) (Tables 9-10). Linguistic-diversity training, by contrast, transfers with almost no penalty (Table 8).
failure_modes: Logical hallucination: the generated conclusion is not logically deducible from the chosen facts (Sec 5.2, manual GPT-4 error analysis).
Distractor confusion: GPT-4 sometimes chooses facts from distractors irrelevant to the proof (Sec 5.2).
Misinterpretation of logical operators, especially double-negation (¬¬) (Sec 5.2).
Under-use of 'unknown': GPT-4 seldom answered 'unknown,' i.e. it answered 'proved' or 'disproved' even when the given facts were insufficient to determine either (Sec 5.2).

Answer/proof unfaithfulness: LLMs 'do not always faithfully follow the logical steps they themselves generate' -- answer accuracy deviates substantially from (stricter) proof accuracy, i.e. models sometimes reach a correct label via an incorrect derivation (Sec 5.2, echoing Turpin et al. 2023; Lanham et al. 2023).

T5 prover error modes fixed by FLD training (Table G.24, EntailmentBank case study): missing/wrong premise selection, 'overclaiming' (including unsupported information in the conclusion, labeled hallucination), repetition of a premise instead of a novel conclusion, and failure to understand negation/conjunction semantics.

Deep-tree collapse under a large, principled rule set: with axioms (A~10 choices/step), accuracy falls to ~0% by depth 6-8 regardless of whether the prover was trained on shallow or deep trees, unlike the implication-only (A=2) setting where shallow-trained provers still generalize somewhat to depth 4-8 (Sec 7.1, Table 7).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's fine-tuned prover is inherently a multistep (stepwise, autoregressive) generator -- it produces one proof step at a time and continues until the hypothesis is (dis-)proved (Sec 4.1) -- and the authors argue this stepwise approach is 'more faithful and robustly generalize[s] to longer proofs' than generating the whole proof at once (Appendix A.3, citing Tafjord et al. 2021; Sanyal et al. 2022; Yang et al. 2022 vs earlier one-shot-generation approaches of Saha et al. 2020; Dalvi et al. 2021). For LLMs in the few-shot in-context setting, the paper finds a genuine forward-pass ceiling: even with 10 demonstrations of the full worked derivation format plus a CoT instruction, GPT-4 answers correctly only about half the time and the underlying proof it generates is frequently invalid -- the model does not reliably chain axioms across many steps in one generation, and 'the prover trained on FLD still struggles with constructing many-step proofs using the axioms' even after gradient-based training (Sec 6.2, Sec 7.1). The paper's core conclusion is that additional specialized training (not just more demonstrations/in-context examples) is needed to acquire deep, many-step deductive competence -- few-shot in-context learning alone does not get LLMs there (Abstract; Sec 5.2: 'additional training specialized in logical reasoning is indeed essential').

relation_to_candidate.position: FLD's fine-tuned-prover protocol is interactive/multi-turn in the sense that the model generates one proof step at a time in a self-continuing loop, observing its own prior generated steps, until it emits a terminal proved/disproved/unknown label (Sec 4.1); this is closer to c18's 'proof engine' machinery than to a plain single-shot classifier, but it is a trained generative model, not an LLM probed zero/few-shot with a single query. FLD's separate LLM few-shot evaluation (GPT-4/3.5/LongAlpaca) is single-shot per instance in the sense that each instance gets one generation (10 demonstrations + one query -> one generated proof + one label), matching c18's 'one query per instance' framing more closely. Output shape in both FLD protocols is richer than c18's: FLD requires the full proof sequence (chosen premises + derived conclusion at each step) in addition to the final proved/disproved/unknown label, and is scored by two metrics -- answer accuracy (label only) and the stricter proof accuracy (label + every intermediate step correct). c18, per its own design notes, deliberately drops full proof-chain generation and keeps only the label to bound output tokens and preserve exact-match scoring. Verifier type: FLD's ground truth comes from the deterministic proof-tree generator itself (a symbolic construction process), and proof accuracy is checked step-by-step against that generated gold tree -- effectively an independent structural check, not merely a definitional flag. This is stronger than c18's noted risk (per c18.md) that its own T/F/Unknown label is 'definitional' (a 50%-probability negation flag) rather than an independently re-derived prover verdict.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c18: Both use fictional/knowledge-free symbolic content (FLD: fully randomized natural-language statements with no semantics, e.g. 'F: run, a: the hamburger'; c18/PrOntoQA: fictional ontology terms like 'wumpus/yumpus') so that pretrained world knowledge cannot substitute for genuine rule-chaining; both use controllable proof/tree depth as the primary difficulty dial (FLD depths 1-8 via forward/backward random deduction, Sec 3.1, Table 2; c18 depth bins D0-D5); both use distractor facts as a second difficulty/robustness dial (FLD's three distractor types -- logical, linguistic, negative-tree, Appendix E.2 -- vs c18's distractor rules); both output one of a small closed label set including an explicit unknown/open-world option (FLD: proved/disproved/unknown; c18: True/False/Unknown); both are held up as contamination-resistant because content is freshly and randomly generated per instance/run.
relation_to_candidate.differences: FLD's deduction rules are the complete axiom set of first-order predicate logic (a designed theoretical property: any valid rule is derivable from them, Gödel completeness, Sec 2), whereas c18 (via PrOntoQA/RuleTaker lineage) uses implication-style if-then rules over a much smaller, non-axiomatic rule inventory -- this is the corpus_claim's central technical distinction and is the paper's own stated point of comparison against RuleTaker-style corpora (Table 1, Sec 1). FLD scores full proof-step sequences (proof accuracy) in its main results, in addition to the final label, and treats the label-only 'answer accuracy' as the more lenient, secondary metric (Sec 4.2); c18 deliberately scores only the final label (per c18.md 'Dead branches'). FLD's headline LLM evaluation is via fine-tuned smaller provers (T5) as the primary object of study, with LLM few-shot evaluation (GPT-4 etc.) as a secondary, smaller-scale analysis (Table 4, 10-shot, 10 different in-context sets) -- c18 is designed around a single scored LLM call per instance as its core protocol, with no fine-tuning loop. FLD's corpus generation pipeline (proof-tree generator + 3 distractor types + template-based NL assignment) is a from-scratch custom build (Appendix E), whereas c18 explicitly reuses the run-verified asaparov/prontoqa codebase rather than reimplementing a generator.
relation_to_candidate.what_c18_would_add: Relative to FLD, c18 would add: (1) a single, bounded, cheaply-scored LLM call per instance (no fine-tuning, no multi-step proof generation to score), which is more suited to a quick, un-optimized baseline; (2) fresh nonce-symbol generation per run via a verified deterministic seed (byte-identical regeneration), addressing FLD's own release-only contamination exposure since FLD's actual corpora are 'available' as fixed released datasets (Appendix F.3) rather than regenerated fresh each run; (3) an independent forward-chaining oracle re-derivation of the label (per c18's own planned mitigation) rather than relying solely on the generator's internal label, addressing the definitional-label risk that FLD does not have (because FLD's proof-accuracy metric already independently checks the full derivation, which c18's design explicitly forgoes for cost reasons); (4) a CSP/seating-puzzle stratum with a unique-solution semantics that FLD's corpora do not cover at all.

corpus_claim: FLD (Morishita et al., ICML 2023) is a principled complete-rule-set generator with proved/disproved/unknown labels under controllable depth and distractors.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper explicitly grounds FLD in the completeness theorem of first-order predicate logic (Sec 2: 'Theorem (Completeness of first-order predicate logic; Gödel, 1930). Any valid deduction rule is derivable by multistep deduction constructed from the axioms...'), builds its corpus generator from that axiom set (Sec 3, Table 1: 'FLD: the axioms (default)'), and each generated example carries a proved/disproved/unknown label (Sec 3.4: 'A deduction example is composed of a set of facts, a hypothesis, a proof sequence, and an answer ("proved", "disproved", or "unknown")'). Depth (proof tree depth 'up to' any value, controlled via forward/backward deduction step counts, Sec 3.1, Table 2 depths 1-3/1-5/1-8) and distractors ('# of Distractors (up to): can be any', Table 1; three distractor types, Appendix E.2) are both explicitly controllable knobs, exactly as the claim states. No correction needed.
baseline_relevant: Dataset scale used in the paper: all corpora had 30,000 training examples, with FLD adding 1,000 validation and 1,000 test examples (Appendix F.1); RuleTaker splits per Tafjord et al. 2021.

Depth dials used: skewed depth distribution 1-3 or 1-5 (biased toward shallow, matching RT(D0-D3)) and uniform depth distribution 1-3, 1-5, or 1-8 (Table 2); the deepest corpus (FLD*/FLD.4, depth up to 8) is 'extremely more challenging' than depth-3 FLD, dropping fine-tuned proof accuracy from 66.4 to 37.7 (Table 3) and few-shot LLM proof accuracy collapsing to near-zero past depth 5-6 (Table 7b).

Distractor dial: up to ~20 distractors per instance across RuleTaker and FLD corpora (Table 2); FLD.0 (0 distractors) vs FLD.2 (~20 distractors) shows distractor training is necessary for robustness (Table 10).

Metrics: 'answer accuracy' (final proved/disproved/unknown label correctness) is the lenient metric; 'proof accuracy' (label + every generated proof step correct, per Saha et al. 2020 with a paper-specific stricter variant) is the metric the authors 'focus discussion on' as the more faithful measure (Sec 4.2). A quick baseline using label-only exact match should expect noticeably higher numbers than any proof-accuracy figures reported here, and should treat 'random guess' (33.3% answer accuracy across three balanced labels, 0.0% proof accuracy, Table 4) as its floor reference.

Ceiling/floor observed for LLMs: GPT-4 10-shot achieves at best ~52.4 answer accuracy (FLD) / ~49.4 answer accuracy (FLD*) with a CoT instruction and 10 demonstrations of the full worked derivation (Table 4); GPT-3.5-Turbo and LongAlpaca-13B are near or at the random-guess floor (0.0-37.6 range, Table 4). This suggests un-optimized single-call LLM baselines on axiom-style/deep synthetic deduction tasks should expect near-chance-to-modest-above-chance performance, not high accuracy, especially at higher depths.

Prompt format that swung results: a chain-of-thought instruction ('Show me a step-by-step thought to the hypothesis based on the given set of facts.') was added to all LLM few-shot prompts (Sec 4.1); the paper does not report a no-CoT ablation for LLMs, so the marginal effect of CoT itself is not isolated in this paper.

Contamination/data-release status: the FLD corpora were released publicly ('The released corpora serve both as learning resources and as challenging benchmarks,' Abstract; footnote in Sec 1 gives https://github.com/hitachi-nlp/FLD; Appendix F.3 states FLD 'will be publicly available'). A corrected version 2 of the corpora was later released fixing a proof-inconsistency bug in version 1 (Appendix H), meaning any external reuse of 'FLD' data should specify which version is meant, and released/fixed instances are static (not regenerated fresh per run), unlike c18's fresh-per-run nonce-symbol regeneration via seeded PrOntoQA.

Rule-set size as the key difficulty lever: the paper's own complexity argument is O(A^d) where A is the number of applicable deduction-rule choices per step and d is depth; RuleTaker-style implication rules have A~2, while FLD's full axiom set has A~10, and the paper attributes essentially all of FLD's extra difficulty over RuleTaker to this combinatorial factor (Sec 5.1, Sec 7.1, Appendix G) -- directly relevant to calibrating a baseline's rule-set breadth as a difficulty dial distinct from depth alone.
quotes: "Even GPT-4 can solve only half of the problems, suggesting that pure logical reasoning isolated from knowledge is still challenging for the LLMs, and additional training specialized in logical reasoning is indeed essential." (Abstract)

"Theorem (Completeness of first-order predicate logic; Gödel, 1930). Any valid deduction rule is derivable by multistep deduction constructed from the axioms. Furthermore, any deduction rule derivable by multistep deduction constructed from the axioms is valid." (Section 2)
"A deduction example is composed of a set of facts, a hypothesis, a proof sequence, and an answer ('proved', 'disproved', or 'unknown')." (Section 3.4)

"We also evaluated large language models in a few-shot in-context learning setting. Specifically, we evaluated GPT-4, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and LongAlpaca-13B... Each in-context example is a pair of a prompt and an output, as illustrated in Figure 1. We also added a chain-of-thought (Kojima et al., 2022) instruction as 'Show me a step-by-step thought to the hypothesis based on the given set of facts.' We ran experiments for 10 different sets of in-context examples." (Section 4.1)
"Interestingly, GPT-4 seldom answered 'unknown', i.e., it answered 'proved' or 'disproved' even if the given facts are not sufficient to either prove or disprove the hypothesis." (Section 5.2)

"However, as Table 7(b) shows, when the corpora use the axioms, neither the provers trained on the shallower tree corpus (FLD.3) nor deeper tree corpus (FLD.4) failed in solving deeper trees." [sic in source, evidently intended 'succeeded' is the opposite reading given the 0.0 percentages that follow; the sentence as extracted literally states 'failed'] (Section 7.1)
"Note that we released the updated, version 2 of FLD corpora, on which the provers perform slightly better (See Appendix H for details)." (Table 3 caption)

"The current official release of FLD corpora is version 2. In version 2, we fixed a proof inconsistency issue of version 1; specifically, the version 1 corpus contained some examples with contradicting proofs, such as 'proved' versus 'disproved.'" (Appendix H)

corrections: Table 4 per-model key_results (models[GPT-4]): the dossier misread the linearized Table 4 row for GPT-4 as 'FLD proof accuracy 12.8, FLD answer accuracy 3.2'. Cross-checking the T5 (fine-tuned) row (75.8, 44.4, 91.6, 72.2) against the source's own Table H.25 (proof accuracy: FLD=75.8, FLD*=44.4) and Table H.26 (answer accuracy: FLD=91.6, FLD*=72.2) shows Table 4's true column order is [proof-acc-FLD, proof-acc-FLD*, answer-acc-FLD, answer-acc-FLD*], not grouped by corpus. So GPT-4's actual values are: proof accuracy FLD=12.8, proof accuracy FLD*=3.2 (not 'FLD answer accuracy'), answer accuracy FLD=52.4, answer accuracy FLD*=49.4. Fixed the key_results text to state the correct metric/corpus pairing.

Table 4 per-model key_results (models[GPT-3.5-Turbo]): same column-misalignment bug -- the dossier's 'FLD 0.0 proof acc / 2.0 answer acc; FLD* 35.8 / 37.6' incorrectly paired 2.0 with FLD's answer accuracy and paired 35.8/37.6 as a FLD*-only pair. Corrected to: proof accuracy FLD=0.0, proof accuracy FLD*=2.0, answer accuracy FLD=35.8, answer accuracy FLD*=37.6.

Table 4 per-model key_results (models[LongAlpaca-13B]): reworded 'FLD 0.0 / 0.0; FLD* 21.2 / 19.6' (which implied a corpus-then-metric grouping) to the verified metric-then-corpus grouping: proof accuracy FLD=0.0, proof accuracy FLD*=0.0, answer accuracy FLD=21.2, answer accuracy FLD*=19.6 (values unchanged since both proof-accuracy entries happen to be 0.0, but the row-pairing description was misleading).

baseline_relevant ceiling/floor entry: corrected 'GPT-4 10-shot achieves at best ~52.4 answer accuracy (FLD*) / ~49.4 (also FLD*)' to '~52.4 answer accuracy (FLD) / ~49.4 answer accuracy (FLD*)' -- 52.4 is FLD's answer accuracy, not a second FLD* figure.

headline_numbers first entry: tightened vague phrasing ('12.8 / low single digits to ~50s') to state the verified figures explicitly (proof accuracy FLD=12.8, FLD*=3.2; answer accuracy FLD=52.4, FLD*=49.4) to prevent the same column-order ambiguity from propagating.
